message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 9 (all tests now implemented and passing)
Recent Green phase: No implementation change — the negative-coordinates test already passed against the existing string-key-based algorithm.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. This is the last per-cycle refactor before the end-refactor pass. If no beneficial change remains, document why.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Let me first read both files to understand the current state.

input.description: Run test suite to confirm green

content.text:
All 9 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the code following the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

I examined every name in the implementation:

- `Cell` — clearly an [x,y] coordinate pair. ✅
- `toKey` / `fromKey` — clearly encode/decode cells as string keys. ✅
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` — clearly the eight surrounding offsets. ✅
- `neighborsOf` — clearly returns surrounding cells. ✅
- `willLive` — reveals the Conway transition decision. ✅
- `countLiveNeighbors` — accurately tallies neighbor counts. ✅
- `nextGeneration` — the public API, clearly named. ✅
- `living` (the Set of live-cell keys) — clear. ✅

All names accurately reveal intent. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

I'll focus on `nextGeneration` (the entry point) plus the helpers, but the most refactor-relevant unit is `nextGeneration`:

```
nextGeneration:
- Bindings: cells (param), living, key, liveNeighbors = 4 → 4
- Invocations: Set(), map, toKey, countLiveNeighbors, spread, filter, willLive, living.has, map, fromKey = 10 → 20
- Loops: 2 (.filter, .map chained) = 10
- Assignments: 0 = 0
Total (nextGeneration): ~34
```

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** ✅ All 9 green.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Code is well-documented and self-explanatory. The string-key approach is a deliberate, clear choice for using coordinates as Map/Set keys.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** I checked carefully. `toKey` is used in two places (building `living` and inside `countLiveNeighbors`), but that is shared use of a single function — not duplication. No copy-paste or conceptual duplication exists.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** The structure is minimal. Each helper serves a distinct, named purpose. No unnecessary abstractions.

## Step 4–5: Refactoring Attempt and Mass Impact

I evaluated several candidate changes:

1. **Inline `fromKey`/`toKey`** — would reduce element count but harm Rule 2 (clarity). The named encode/decode pair documents the key scheme. Rejected (Rule 2 trumps APP).
2. **Switch from string keys to a numeric Cantor-pairing key** — would avoid string allocation, but adds arithmetic complexity and harms readability for no behavioral benefit. Rejected (Rule 2 trumps APP).
3. **Use a `for...of` loop over the Map instead of the `[...map].filter().map()` chain** — this is a viable micro-change but would *increase* element count (an explicit `result` array + `push` assignment, mass +6 per assignment) and reduce the declarative clarity. Rejected.

I made one small, beneficial change: the `living` Set is logically a set of *keys*, and the intermediate `[...countLiveNeighbors(cells)]` spread materializes an array purely to chain `.filter().map()`. I considered renaming `living` to `liveCellKeys` to make explicit that it holds keys, not cells — this strengthens Rule 2 (reveals intent) at zero mass cost.

   Start at  21:49:57
   Duration  152ms (transform 24ms, setup 0ms, collect 23ms, tests 4ms, environment 0ms, prepare 42ms)

content.text:
All 9 tests still green after the change.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied** (one change, Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):
- Renamed local `living` → `liveCellKeys`. The variable holds a `Set<string>` of *encoded keys*, not `Cell` objects. The old name suggested it held live cells; the new name makes the type/contents explicit and matches how it is used (`liveCellKeys.has(key)`).

**Mass impact:** Δ 0 — pure rename, no elements added or removed. This is the correct outcome: Rule 2 (clarity) is improved at zero APP cost, and Rule 2 takes priority over APP regardless.

**Changes considered and rejected** (documented per process):
- Inlining `toKey`/`fromKey` — would lower element count but destroy the self-documenting encode/decode boundary (Rule 2 trumps APP).
- Numeric/Cantor-pairing keys instead of strings — micro-optimization that adds arithmetic complexity for no behavioral or clarity gain (Rule 2 trumps APP).
- Replacing the declarative `[...map].filter().map()` chain with an imperative `for...of` + `push` — would *increase* mass (adds an assignment, mass +6) and reduce clarity. Rejected.

## Step 7: Report

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring:** Renamed local variable `living` → `liveCellKeys` in `nextGeneration` to reveal that it stores encoded string keys rather than `Cell` tuples (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent).
- **Mass Change:** ~34 → ~34 (Δ 0; pure rename, clarity improved at no APP cost).
- **Tests:** All 9 passing ✅

File: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_21-37-39_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`

The implementation was already in strong shape from prior refactor cycles — well-named helpers, no duplication, minimal elements, and a clear declarative `nextGeneration`. The only beneficial change remaining was the intent-revealing rename. Deeper structural changes (key-scheme swaps, loop rewrites) were evaluated and rejected because they would trade clarity for mass without behavioral benefit. This leaves the code well-positioned for the upcoming end-refactor pass.
